title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 7)
text:
-- 3 of 58 --

2024-2025 Fire Technology
Program Review
5
I. Who are we?
The United States Fire Service is a diverse industry offering many career paths. In California,
there is a rigorous set of requirements candidates must meet to qualify for work within the
state's fire service jurisdictions. The College of the Siskiyous is one of the select few Accredited
Regional Training Programs (ARTP) a Division of The Office of the State Fire Marshal (OSFM).
The College of the Siskiyous Fire Technology program is under the Career and Technical
Education (CTE) Program that prepares students for various careers in the fire service,
including roles such as Professional Firefighters, Volunteer Firefighters, and Wildland
Firefighters. The curriculum also supports students pursuing an Associate of Science (AS)
degree. All programs meet local, state, and federal standards.
Key program offerings are in 3 categories:
1. Fire / Emergency Response Technology (AS) Courses
o The 6 required classes are grouped into what we call our Core classes.
2. Fire Academies
o State Firefighter I & II Fire Academy
(offered in Spring and Fall)
o Cal Fire Wildland 1C (offered in
Spring and Summer, non-credit, dual
enrollment)
3. Stand Alone Classes
o Specialized training tracks in
Firefighter II, Rescue, HazMat,
Company Officer, Instructor, Wildland Firefighter, and Command.
Students at the College of the Siskiyous can earn a Certificate of Completion for Company
Officer or an Associate of Science Degree Fire / Emergency Response Technology, which can
be transferred to a California State University. The college also provides opportunities for
professional firefighters to return for continuing education, enabling them to meet
requirements for promotions or specialized positions within their organizations.
The goal of these programs is to equip students with the knowledge and qualifications needed
to remain competitive in today's highly impacted fire service industry.

-- 4 of 58 --

2024-2025 Fire Technology
Program Review
6
A. Fire Technology Organizational Structure
The Fire Technology Program is overseen by Veronica Coots, Interim Dean of Career and
Technical Education (CTE). The program includes the following Academic Affairs Staff
positions:
Full-Time Administrative Support – CTE
• Monique Gonsalez – Administrative Assistant III - CTE
• Anne Marie Acord – Administrative Assistant I - CTE
Full-Time Faculty - District Funded
• Chris Delcour - Fire Technology Coordinator/Instructor
• Jesse Roberts - Fire Technology Instructor
Part-Time Faculty & Expert Instructional Aids (EIA)
Part-Time Adjunct Instructors: Approximately 40 part-time
adjunct instructors contribute significantly to our Fire Program
by training the next generation, while also working full-time as
professional firefighters. Fire Instructors must meet both State
Fire Training Standards and Community College Standards. See
Appendix A for requirements.
Expert Instructional Aids (EIA): Approximately 20 part-time EIAs support the Adjunct
Instructors and Fire Technology Staff with daily operations. EIAs are Firefighters who are
working on becoming both State Fire Training and COS qualified.

B. What is our Primary Purpose?
The purpose of the Fire Technology Program is to provide educational opportunities to our
students, so they are successful in their individual goals. In addition to offering the
fundamental classes required to begin a career in the fire service, the College of the
Siskiyous Fire Technology Program has a huge demand and enrollment in classes that the
state of California requires for promotional opportunities and specialized positions within
the fire department the
students serve. We see a
large number of new
enrollment numbers for
these classes since we
are one of very few
institutions that offer
these courses.

To further diversify enrollment, it is crucial to implement strategies that promote inclusivity
and equal opportunities for all ethnic groups. This can include:
• Educational Programs: Offering cultural competency training and diversity education to
foster understanding and respect among different ethnic groups.
• Community Engagement: Encouraging participation from all ethnic communities in local
events and decision-making processes to ensure their voices are heard and valued.
• Supportive Policies: Developing policies that address the unique needs of
underrepresented groups, ensuring they have access to resources and opportunities for
growth and development.
By actively working towards greater diversity, we can create a more inclusive and
harmonious community where everyone feels valued and respected.

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 10)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
13
D. What Services do we provide?
The Fire Technology Program offers a comprehensive range of courses tailored to both high
school students and adults, aiming to provide pathways for certifications, degrees, career
advancement, and professional development. Currently, we offer the following Fire
Technology Emergency Response Technology AS and Certificate of Completion - Company
Officer
The curriculum meets or exceeds the standards required for employment in local
government fire departments, whether volunteer or paid professionals, as well as
municipal, state (Cal Fire), and United States Forest Services (USFS). The program is aligned
with national standards, including those set by the National Fire Protection Association
(NFPA), National Wildfire Coordinating Group (NWCG), California Specialized Training
Institute (CSTI), International Fire Service Accreditation Congress (IFSAC), and Pro Board.
Additionally, it ensures compliance with California State Fire Training (SFT) guidelines,
providing students with the necessary competencies to meet the following standards:
• Company Officer - SFT
• Fire Apparatus Driver/Operator – SFT
• Fire Fighter 1 – SFT
• Fire Fighter 2 – SFT
• Fire Inspector – SFT
• Fire Investigator - SFT
• Technical Rescue Specialist Classes – SFT
• Hazmat Specialist Classes – SFT & CSTI
• Wildland Classes – NWCG

-- 12 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 11)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
14
E. How do we deliver our services?
The Fire Technology classes are grouped into 4 basic categories. Classes are delivered in a
combination of classroom lectures and lab/drill ground skills practicing. Classes are normally
done at the Weed Technical Training Center, but when needed, the classes will use offsite
facilities or locations to meet certain student outcomes. Another benefit of taking the class
to remote locations, we can reach students who may not be able to travel. Many of our
students are working Firefighters who cannot leave the area they protect.
1. Fire Academy – Firefighter 1 & 2
SFT Academy is a 24-week Vocational program that certifies the
Cadet as a Firefighter 1 and Firefighter 2 with IFSAC and ProBoard
Certifications. This is a degree-specific course required for the Fire
Emergency Response Technology AS Degree. Scholarships and
Financial Aid are often used to assist with tuition. SMART Workforce also offers support
for those who qualify. YouTube Link to Video of Fire Academy 45
2. Core Classes
The 6 Fire Technology Degree-specific courses are
required for the Fire Emergency Response Technology
AS Degree. Financial Aid is often used to assist with the
fees. These Core classes are now offered on a 2
Semester cycle with half being taught in person and the
other half being taught online. This allows our students
to complete the required degree classes within a 2-
semester cycle, either online or in person. We are proud to announce for the first time
that Fire Technology is offering Distant Education.
3. Stand Alone Classes
Our stand-alone classes are vocational courses. The Course
Plan is normally dictated by the following organizations,
SFT, NWCG, FEMA, and CSTI. We also have classes that are
developed by our instructors to meet certain requested
needs. These classes are normally about 1 week and
financial aid is not used.
4. Non-Credit classes
Over the last year, we have pursued a dual enrollment course to reach our high school
students. This course, in partnership with CalFire Siskiyou Unit, provides High School
Seniors with the basic skills needed to be hired by a Cal Fire professional hand crew. This
is a category of classes we are continuing to explore.

-- 13 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 12)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
15
F. How we keep the program current
Our curriculum is meticulously updated to align with the needs of our accredited
organizations and the evolving standards within the field. The update cycle and the
standard are determined by California State Fire Training every 3–5 years. This standard
is designed to match the requirements of the National Fire Protection Association
(NFPA), the National Wildfire Coordinating Group (NWCG), California State Fire Training,
and the California Community Colleges (CCC) system, whichever is shorter.
The extensive range of classes offered by the COS Fire Technology program demands
significant effort from our staff to remain current. By consistently updating our
knowledge and adhering to the latest standards, we guarantee
top-quality education and training for our students. Being active
members of the California Fire Technology Directors Association
(CFTDA) and attending quarterly meetings, has been an
invaluable resource for the most current information available.
To ensure the delivery of a high-quality Fire Technology
Program, substantial investment in equipment and consumables
is essential. Much of this funding is sourced from the Strong
Workforce (SWF) budget, thereby not impacting district funds.
Over the past few years, we have made significant investments
to modernize the program by updating outdated equipment and
training props. Appendix J – Equipment Props
G. What courses do we offer?
The College of the Siskiyous Fire Technology Program offers a comprehensive curriculum
tailored to meet the evolving needs and stringent requirements of Fire Departments across
the state. Each semester, we ensure the provision of at least one State Fire Academy and a
minimum of three core classes online and 3 core classes in person, with a 2-semester
alternating schedule.
Our course offerings are further expanded based on direct input from our collaborative Fire
Advisory Committee, and our local Fire Departments, with Cal Fire being our primary
mutual assistance agency due to their high demand for ongoing firefighter training.
Our program stands out among community colleges due to our unique ability to provide
stand-alone classes that fulfill specific promotional and specialized training mandates set by
the State Fire Marshal. This distinctive feature is made possible by our dedicated pool of

-- 14 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 13)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
16
California State-Certified Adjunct Instructors, whose expertise is pivotal in executing these
advanced educational demands. Our instructors must go through a rigorous path of
education to be certified by the State Fire Marshal.
In addition to responding to student inquiries and recertification needs, we also tailor our
offerings based on instructor availability—further evidence of our commitment to
adaptability and excellence in fire service education.
While eLumen provides a representation of our approximate 60 courses, it should be noted
that this does not encompass our full range; we also offer the ability to tailor a class for a
specific need and have the potential to add to our catalog. Both NWCG and SFT have more
classes that could be added as we expand and grow. Click to See Appendix B – Catalog of
Courses

-- 15 of 58 --

2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
17
H. Enrollment Trends
The Fire Technology Program at the College of the Siskiyous has demonstrated a dynamic
trend in student enrollment over the past six academic years, as illustrated by the Full-Time
Equivalent Students (FTES) chart.
FTE’s Earned per Year
Enrollment Trends:
• 2018-2019 to 2019-2020: The program maintained a stable enrollment, reflecting
consistent interest and participation from students.
• 2019-2020 to 2020-2021: A noticeable decline in FTES occurred, likely influenced by the
global COVID-19 pandemic, which impacted many educational institutions and
programs.
• 2020-2021 to 2021-2022: The program began to recover, showing a gradual increase in
student enrollment as the college adapted to new learning environments and safety
protocols.
• 2021-2022 to 2022-2023: A significant surge in FTES was observed, reaching the highest
enrollment in the six-year period. This peak indicates a robust recovery and growing

-- 16 of 58 --

2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
18
interest in the Fire Technology Program. It should also be noted that the Full-Time
Academic staff was increased to 2.
• 2022-2023 to 2023-2024: While there is a slight decrease from the previous peak, the
enrollment remains strong, suggesting sustained interest and the program’s resilience.
Analysis and Implications: The fluctuating enrollment numbers highlight the program’s
ability to adapt and thrive despite external challenges. The peak in 2022-2023 suggests
successful outreach and program enhancements that attracted a higher number of
students. It should be noted that we can directly relate the enrollment success to our
Academic Staff increase to 2 Full-Time Staff Members, instead of the prior 1. For Fire
Technology to continue to grow and expand, it is imperative that we receive additional
staffing. Our staff's current workload is unsustainable to continue into the future. Moving
forward, it is essential to analyze the factors contributing to these trends to maintain and
further increase enrollment.

Appendix I -High School Transitioning Count

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 14)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
19
I. Strategic Recommendations
1. Enhanced Outreach: Continue and expand outreach efforts to prospective students,
emphasizing the program’s strengths and career opportunities in fire technology.
2. Program Development: Invest in curriculum updates and new technologies to keep the
program relevant and appealing.
3. Support Services: Strengthen student support services to ensure high retention and
success rates, particularly during challenging times.
4. Community Partnerships: Foster partnerships with local fire departments and
emergency services to provide students with practical experience and job placement
opportunities.
5. Additional Instructor: We are recommending adding 1 full-time Instructor to oversee
the Fire Academy. By adding this additional instructor, we would have the bandwidth to
run 2 Fire Academy’s each year.
6. Additional Administrative Staff: We are seeing an increase in administrative work as
our numbers increase. This is putting a strain on our Admin staff; we are concerned that
they will not continually handle the increasing workload. We are requesting
administrative staff to be assigned primarily to Fire Technology.
7. Equipment Manager – CTE: This position has been approved by the prior Dean Mark
Klever, and the process was started over a year ago to get this position flown and filled.
By not filling this position it is pulling time away from the instructors that could be
better used expanding the program and solving larger problems.

-- 18 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 15)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
20
II. External Factors that may affect our program
A. Budgetary
The College of the Siskiyous Fire Technology Program has experienced significant growth
over recent years, despite facing several external challenges. This narrative outlines the key
external factors influencing our program, including budget constraints and reductions in
strong workforce program (SWP) funding.
1. Budget Constraints
Our program has seen a steady increase in enrollment, reflected in the growing interest
and need for skilled professionals in fire technology. However, this growth has occurred
without a corresponding increase in our budget. The lack of additional financial
resources has necessitated innovative approaches to maintain the quality of education
and training we provide. We have optimized our existing resources, leveraged
partnerships, and sought alternative funding sources to ensure our students receive the
best possible education.
2. Reduction in State Workforce Funding
The recent announcement of the decrease in the state’s SWP funding will impose a
significant challenge for moving forward. This funding was crucial in supporting various
aspects of our program, including equipment upgrades and replacement, upgrading,
and purchasing props, new equipment to expand our catalog of classes, and marketing.
We have been able to use SWP money to support our program without raising our
budget, but we have concerns that if this funding dries up, our program will not be able
to sustain these essential components needed for us to continue. Despite these financial
constraints, our commitment to producing highly skilled fire technology professionals
remains unwavering.
3. Increased Demand for Fire Technology Professionals
The demand for fire technology professionals has surged due to the increasing
frequency and severity of wildfires. This heightened demand underscores the
importance of our program and the need for continued investment in training and
education. We are actively engaging with industry partners and community stakeholders
to advocate for the necessary resources to support our program’s growth and address
the critical need for trained professionals in this field.
4. Strategic Partnerships and Community Support
In response to these challenges, we have strengthened our partnerships with local fire
departments, industry leaders, and community organizations. These collaborations have
been instrumental in providing our students with hands-on training opportunities,

-- 19 of 58 --

2023-2024 Fire Technology

21
internships, and job placements. Additionally, community support has been vital in
advocating for the importance of our program and securing additional resources.

The following chart displays instructional hours provided and paid for by the Fire
Warden Program & CalFire in various training classes over 2023-2024. The blue bars
represent the hours for each specific training, while the orange line indicates the
cumulative "Total Hours" across all categories. The chart shows an overall increase in
total instructional hours, with a notable rise across categories, highlighting the Fire
Warden Program's expanding support for college-registered classes through its
partnership.
6. Conclusion:
The College of the Siskiyous Fire Technology Program continues to thrive despite
external challenges. Our ability to adapt and innovate in the face of budget constraints
and reduced SWP funding is a testament to our dedication to excellence in fire
technology education. We remain committed to preparing our students for successful
careers and meeting the growing demand for skilled professionals in this critical field.

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 17)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
22
B. Competition:
The nearest Fire program is approximately 70
miles to the South of us and their recent
expansion with CalFire could affect our
enrollment and future growth. We also have
competition to the East in Lassen which is 139
miles away. We do not feel a strong impact
from their program.
C. Requirements of four-year
institutions
From our research, transfer to any other four-year degree program may vary slightly
depending on the College selected. But generally, the items required are:
1. Complete Fire Technology Courses: Ensure you have completed foundational
courses in fire technology, such as fire prevention, fire behavior, and emergency
medical services.
2. General Education Requirements: Fulfill the general education requirements, which
typically include courses in English, math, science, and social sciences.
3. Maintain a Strong GPA: Aim for a competitive GPA, often around 2.5 to 3.0 or
higher, depending on the institution and program.
4. Earn an Associate Degree: While not always required, earning an Associate Degree
in Fire Technology or a related field can facilitate the transfer process.
5. Transferable Credits: Work with your academic advisor to ensure that the courses
you take are transferable to the four-year college you plan to attend.
6. Meet Specific Program Requirements: Some fire service programs may have
additional prerequisites, such as certifications or specific coursework. Check the
requirements of the program you’re interested in.
D. Institutional Regulations, policies, standards, and mandates
California fire academies are accredited by California State Fire Training and must undergo a
reaccreditation process every five years through the State Fire Training (SFT) division. This
process involves a comprehensive review of the academy’s programs, facilities, and
instructional methods to ensure they continue to meet the high standards set by the SFT,
IFSAC, and ProBoard.
1. California Health and Safety Code (HSC): Authorizes activities under the State Fire
Training (SFT) program, including regulations for fire service training and education.
2. Title 19, California Code of Regulations: Outlines the California Fire Service Training and
Education Program, detailing standards and procedures for fire training.

-- 21 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 18)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
23
3. State Fire Training Procedures Manual: Documents administrative regulations and
provides a framework for SFT’s training and education programs.
4. Certification Training Standards (CTS): Developed by State Fire Training, these
standards outline qualifications for various fire service roles, based on national
standards like NFPA 1021 and NFPA 1051.
5. IFSAC and ProBoard Accreditation: The International Fire Service Accreditation
Congress (IFSAC) and the National Board on Fire Service Professional Qualifications
(ProBoard) provide accreditation for fire service training programs, ensuring they meet
national standards.
6. NFPA Standards: The National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) sets various standards
that fire academies must adhere to, such as NFPA 1001 for Firefighter Professional
Qualifications and NFPA 1021 for Fire Officer Professional Qualifications.
7. NWCG Standards: The National Wildfire Coordinating Group (NWCG) establishes
standards for wildland fire position qualifications, including training, experience,
physical fitness, and currency requirements. These standards ensure that personnel are
qualified for national mobilization to wildland fire incidents.
8. Local and Federal Regulations: Compliance with local and federal regulations, including
those set by agencies like CalFire and the US Forest Service (USFS), which may have
additional training and operational standards.
9. Fire Academy Instructor Qualifications:
• Fire Instructors must meet California State Fire Training Minimum requirements
to teach at the Fire Academy. The Following State Fire Training classes must be
completed to be available to teach at the ARTP Fire Academy.
1. “Instructor 1” - 40-hour class
2. Ethical Leadership in the Classroom - 8 hours
3. Certified as a Firefighter 1 and or 2
• Must also meet College of the Siskiyous Adjunct Instructor Minimum
Requirements.
These elements ensure that fire academies in California provide high-quality training
and education, preparing graduates for effective roles in fire protection and emergency
response.
E. Job Market
Job Market Analysis for Firefighter Jobs in the North State of California
a) Projected Growth
The job market for firefighters in the North State of California is expected to grow
by 4% from 2021 to 2026. This growth rate is slightly higher than the national
average for firefighter job growth, which is projected at 3.6% from 2022 to 2032.
This indicates a steady demand for firefighters in the region, driven by the need for
fire protection and emergency response services.

-- 22 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 19)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
24
b) Annual Job Openings
On average, there are approximately 111 annual job openings for firefighters in
Northern California. These openings result from both new positions created due to
growth and replacements for retiring or transitioning workers. However, we
believe this figure may be underestimated due to CalFire’s increased efforts to hire
more firefighters to accommodate a new working schedule. While we cannot
project an exact number, discussions with our local partners support this
assessment.
c) Current Job Market Overview
The North State of California, encompassing counties such as Butte, Colusa, Del
Norte, Glenn, Humboldt, Lake, Lassen, Mendocino, Modoc, Plumas, Shasta, Sierra,
Siskiyou, Tehama, and Trinity, has a robust demand for public safety professionals,
including firefighters. As of 2021, there were approximately 1,343 firefighter jobs
in this region.
d) Wages
The average firefighter salary in the North State of California can vary depending on
the specific county and the level of experience. Based on the available data and
regional reports, here are some insights:
1. Butte County: The average salary for firefighters is approximately
$70,000 to $90,000 per year.
2. Shasta County: Firefighters earn an average salary ranging from $65,000
to $85,000 per year.
3. Humboldt County: The average salary for firefighters is around $60,000
to $80,000 per year.
4. Siskiyou County: Firefighters in this county typically earn between
$55,000 and $75,000 per year.
5. Tehama County: The average salary for firefighters is approximately
$60,000 to $80,000 per year1.
These figures represent a general range and can vary based on factors such as
specific fire departments, additional qualifications, and years of experience. The
cost of living in these areas also influences the salary ranges.

-- 23 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 20)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
25
e) Key Employers and Job Postings
The top employers for firefighter positions in the North State of California include
local government agencies, state government, and federal agencies such as the
U.S. Forest Service. Job postings for firefighters are relatively frequent, with a
posting intensity of 2:1, indicating that employers are actively seeking to fill these
positions.
• Skills and Qualifications
Employers in the region look for candidates with specialized skills in fire
suppression, emergency medical services, and hazardous materials
handling. Additionally, soft skills such as communication, teamwork, and
problem-solving are highly valued1. Most firefighter positions require a
postsecondary nondegree award, such as completion of a fire academy
program.
• Future Outlook
The future outlook for firefighter jobs in the North State of California
remains positive. The region’s ongoing development and the increasing
frequency of wildfires necessitate a well-trained and adequately staffed
fire service. The National Wildfire Coordinating Group (NWCG) standards
and certifications, along with IFSAC and ProBoard accreditations, ensure
that firefighters are well-prepared to meet these challenges.
• Conclusion
Overall, the job market for firefighters in the North State of California is
expected to remain strong over the next few years. With steady job
growth, competitive wages, and a consistent demand for skilled
professionals, firefighting continues to be a vital and rewarding career in
this region.

-- 24 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 21)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
26
F. Population:
As of the 2020 census, Siskiyou County, California, has a population of
approximately 44,900. This obviously leads to limited enrollment from our local high school
students. There is, however, significant interest from students in other areas of the state.
This element is limited to our housing situation. With our Academy now beginning in the
middle of the Fall Semester, many of the dorm rooms are occupied by this time. There are
also very few rental opportunities in the local area.
Student High School Data of the 718 Students is located in Appendix I
Appendix K – Census Demographics Data Siskiyous County
G. Standalone Classes
Many of our students who come for a 1-Week Standalone are forced to rent a hotel room
for this period. These costs are typically not covered by their own fire departments and are
paid out-of-pocket. Having on-site housing strictly for CTE students may relieve this added
obstacle and attract more students into our standalone programs.
H. Marketing
Marketing our students and the classes we offer is a high priority. We continuing to find
marketing strategies to label the College of the Siskiyous as a sole organization to offer
students all the requirements to be successful in their careers must be a high priority.
In the last couple of years, we have been utilizing Constant Contact to advertise the Fire
Technology classes and have built a data base of over 1000 contacts. We have also been
utilizing Fire Nuggets a popular website for Firefighter training and have improved our social
media platform with Facebook, Instagram and YouTube.

-- 25 of 58 --

2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
27

Campaign was directed towards program advertisement. In collaboration with Signarama;
Fire Technology, Law Enforcement Academy, and EMS all came together on an exciting
project. This initiative focused on creating a
more engaging, professional, and visually

in the program, showcasing the professionalism and
dedication required in fire technology and public safety
fields.

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 22)
text:
-- 26 of 58 --

2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
28
I. Location & Pay
1. Our Fire Technology program, located in the remote area of Weed, California, faces
unique challenges in recruiting and retaining qualified instructors from outside of
Siskiyou County and neighboring areas. Our remote location means that many of
our instructors travel significant distances, often incurring personal expenses for
hotel accommodations. Providing additional dormitory facilities for these
instructors would alleviate this burden and make our program more attractive to
potential instructors.
2. Additionally, our program’s Adjunct Instructor compensation is low compared to
similar community college programs in central and southern parts of the state. This
disparity further complicates our efforts to attract and retain qualified instructors.
Currently, the majority of our adjunct instructors are local, but their qualifications
to teach specialized standalone classes are limited.
3. Expanding our roster of qualified adjunct instructors is crucial. Doing so will
significantly enhance our program offerings, directly boosting enrollment and
increasing the demand for our program. Addressing these challenges is essential for
the continued growth and success of our Fire Technology program.
III. What is the progress on our Outcomes Assessment?
• SLO’s evaluated each semester and evaluated for alignment with programmatic SLO’s
• The programs have been updated to meet State Fire requirements, equipment has been
updated to meet those standards and future needs are being addressed
• Continual program and course assessment throughout the next four years

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 24)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
32
IV. Description of our Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
The institutional program effectiveness indicators focus on staffing, student success, and
curriculum alignment with state and national standards. Staffing remains a critical factor, with a
growing reliance on adjunct instructors. The program currently operates with two full-time
faculty members, supplemented by part-time instructors actively working in the fire profession.
However, to support future growth, an increase in full-time staff is necessary. Expanding the
roster of qualified adjunct instructors will enhance program offerings and ensure sustained
student success. The program's continued alignment with State Fire Training and National
Wildfire Coordinating Group standards ensures that graduates meet the qualifications required
for various fire service roles.
Student success is consistently tracked across multiple semesters, highlighting both challenges
and growth opportunities. Enrollment trends show a steady increase in headcount for both the
fall and spring terms, reflecting a growing interest in the Fire Technology Program. Success
rates are also tied to ongoing outcomes assessment, with student learning outcomes evaluated
regularly to align with programmatic goals. Equipment and curriculum are continuously
updated to meet state fire requirements, ensuring that students receive relevant, real-world
training that prepares them for immediate entry into the field.
A. Success by Modality
This chart displays student success rates by instructional modality (Distance Education, In-
Person, and Other) across multiple academic years. In-person classes show consistently high
success rates, fluctuating between 93.2% and 99.2% from 2016-2024. Distance education
has maintained a stable success rate of around 83% in the latest years. However, the
"Other" instructional category shows significant volatility, with a sharp drop from 100% in
2020-2021 to 40% in 2021-2022, before partially recovering to 58.8% in 2022-2023. Overall,
in-person instruction consistently outperforms other modes, while distance education
remains steady but lower.

-- 31 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 25)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
33
V. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research:

various major categories for the academic year 2023-2024. It breaks down the student
enrollments as follows:

students

-- 32 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 26)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
34
VI. Overall Evaluation
The Program Review 2023-2024 for the College of the Siskiyous Fire Technology Program offers
a thorough evaluation of its current operations, challenges, and future objectives, focusing on
maintaining the program’s reputation as a leader in fire service education.
A. Program Overview
The College of the Siskiyous Fire Technology Program is a comprehensive training program
designed to equip students for various fire service careers, including professional and
volunteer firefighters, wildland firefighters, career advancement, and those seeking
advanced certification. The program operates under strict adherence to local, state, and
federal standards, providing both certificates and an Associate of Science (AS) degree in Fire
and Emergency Response Technology.
B. Organizational Structure
The program is managed by the Interim Dean of Career and Technical Education (CTE),
Veronica Coots, and supported by full-time faculty and approximately 40 part-time adjunct
instructors who are also active professionals in the fire service. The program's instructional
aids (EIAs) provide critical support to adjunct instructors. The curriculum is updated every 3-
5 years to align with industry standards set by the National Fire Protection Association
(NFPA), the National Wildfire Coordinating Group (NWCG), and California State Fire
Training.
C. Enrollment and Demographics
Student enrollment in the program has seen steady growth, particularly over the last two
years, with the program's success tied to expanded course offerings and adding additional
full-time faculty support. Continual efforts are being made to diversify enrollments and
increase female and underrepresented groups. There has also been notable demand from
working firefighters returning for professional development for promotions.
D. Program Offerings
The program provides a wide array of training, including State Firefighter I and II Academies,
core degree courses, and specialized standalone classes like HazMat, Company Officer, and
Technical Rescue training. Delivery is flexible, with both in-person and online classes,
accommodating both full-time students and working professionals. High school dual-
enrollment programs are being created to introduce younger students to the fire service.
E. External Challenges and Strategic Needs
Despite its growth, the program faces significant challenges, particularly in funding. The
recent reduction in strong workforce funding (SWF) will put pressure on resources for

-- 33 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 27)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
35
equipment and facility upgrades. There is also competition from nearby institutions, and the
program is limited by its geographic location, housing capacity, and lack of adequate
facilities such as dedicated dorms for students and a Fire Apparatus Bay to protect essential
vehicles and equipment. These constraints need to be addressed to sustain the program’s
growth and ability to meet industry demands.
F. Strategic Recommendations
Key recommendations for the future include increasing full-time faculty to meet the
growing demand for courses, expanding facilities to support both students and instructors,
and developing additional partnerships with local fire departments to provide practical
training and employment opportunities.
G. Conclusion
The Fire Technology Program at the College of the Siskiyous is a vital component of fire
service education in Northern California, producing highly trained professionals in a field
where demand continues to rise. However, to remain competitive and to continue
providing high-quality education, the program must address its funding, staffing, and
facility challenges. By investing in strategic improvements, the program can continue to
grow and meet the evolving needs of the fire service industry, ensuring its long-term
success and sustainability.
VII. What is our Vision?
The program seeks to stay ahead of job market interest, particularly the increased demand for
Firefighters. To do this, the program aims to provide students with up-to-date training using
state-of-the-art props and equipment. The program has already acquired many updated pieces
of equipment, including a digital fire training simulator, a smoke machine, new fire nozzles, new
hand tools, fire hose, rope rescue equipment, rescue dummies, and more.
The program offers the following Degree Certificate:
• Fire/Emergency Response Technology AS Degree (AS)
• Company Officer Certificate of Completion (COC)
• Fire Fighter 1 and 2 Academy (IFSAC & ProBoard)
We are in the planning/investigation stages of adding additional Degrees and Certificates of
Achievement offered in other Community College Programs. This may lead to adding more
elective Fire Technology classes. We currently offer most of the classes to complete the
following:
• Fire Technology / Company Officer AS Degree (AS)
• Fire Technology / Degree Wildland AS Degree (AS)
• Fire Technology Certificate of Achievement (COS)

-- 34 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 28)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
36
Here are some key aspects of the program's vision for the future:
A. Increased Staffing:
There has been a direct improvement across all measurable markers since the full-time staff
has been increased to Two Staff Members. The Fire Technology staff is working at its
maximum capacity at present. For the program to continue to grow we will need to Increase
full-time staffing.
The program sees the need for an additional full-time Instructor to primarily oversee the
Fire Academies. This will allow the Fire Technology Coordinator to continue to focus on
expanding and managing the program. We are also in dire need of the Instructional Support
specialist position flown and filled. The College has a huge financial investment in fire
equipment and props that need to be maintained. This position helps maintain CTE
equipment and facilities.
The sources also include a list of the program's prior goals, and the progress made toward
achieving those goals. For example, the program has successfully increased full-time staffing
from one to two instructors. The program has also acquired new equipment, updated its
website, and developed distance education capabilities.
B. Training Grounds:
The program will continue to build out its training grounds with new and upgraded props.
The priority is to have the propane fire props upgraded in the Weed Tower. They are more
than 20 years old, and the system broke over a year ago and parts to repair it are no longer
available. The cost to upgrade the props is an estimated $400,000. We continue to
investigate Grants but have been unsuccessful.
C. Facilities:
The program sees the need for an apparatus housing facility as a high priority. The program
has three fire engines stored outside. Exposure to weather elements causes severe damage
to our vehicles. The program wants to begin construction on a Fire Apparatus Bay with the
potential to expand it into a training fire station. The fire station would house equipment
and provide a realistic training environment. It may include classrooms, dorm housing, and
a locker room. The program also recognizes the need for additional classrooms and
permanent facilities to meet the demand of growing enrollment and stand-alone programs.
We often must scramble to find classrooms for our stand-alone classes.
Cal Fire has recently approached us about the possibility of doing a long-term lease on
office space for their training and investigation bureau. We have started meetings to see if
this is possible for the College. We see many advantages to this partnership and strongly
encourage this project to be completed.

-- 35 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 29)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
37
D. Partnership with EMS and Administration of Justice Programs:
The program plans to continue exploring a joint partnership with the EMS and
Administration of Justice programs to enhance collaboration and sharing of resources. A
long-term goal is to merge the programs into a single area on campus, creating more space
and allowing students to train together. We have a vision of a Public Safety portion of the
Campus with the back gate being the main entrance.
VIII. Progress on Prior Goals
A. Increased Staffing Levels
• We have successfully added more standalone classes, which has increased the workload
both in the classroom and on the administrative side. To continue expanding, it is crucial
to receive the new positions we are requesting. We have not been successful in getting
our positions posted and or filled.
B. Acquisition of New Equipment
• Significant progress has been made in acquiring new hand tools, hoses, nozzles, and
props. This priority ensures our students train with the same equipment currently used
in the fire service.
C. Website Updates
• The Fire Technology website has been updated with current material, including a
calendar of our standalone classes, and is being maintained regularly.
• We have integrated the Podium software platform into our website, allowing students
to text us directly for quick responses. It also serves as a communication tool for all Fire
Technology staff.
D. Distance Education Capabilities
• Five instructors are now trained in Canvas, enabling us to offer distance education.
• We have successfully offered our core classes online on a two-year alternating schedule
with in-person sessions.
• Additionally, we have started offering standalone hybrid classes.
E. Curriculum Updates
• Progress has been made in updating the curriculum in eLumen. We will continue
working in this area to stay current.

-- 36 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 30)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
38
F. Apparatus Bay Expansion
• CTE and Facilities have initiated the process of building a larger apparatus bay for
storing all CTE vehicles and equipment. However, this process stalled due to higher costs
than what Strong Workforce funds could support. This project will need to be included
in the district budget, either partially or fully.
G. Joint Training Facilities with EMS and ADJ
• This long-term goal aims to merge our programs into a localized area on campus, freeing
up vital space and supporting our students in learning to work together in a public
safety team environment. No progress has been made yet.
H. Housing Opportunities for CTE Students
• Students have raised concerns about sharing housing with students from different
programs on varying schedules, which affects their study and rest. We are exploring the
possibility of using the old dorms for CTE students after the new dorms are built or
allocating a section of the new dorms for CTE students.
I. Additional Training Fire Station
• This proposed joint program with local fire departments would support a working fire
station on campus, providing on-the-job training for our students and enhancing EMS
and fire protection for the campus and local area. This initiative could potentially lower
the college’s insurance costs and serve as a recruitment tool. We recommend exploring
organizations with existing infrastructure to determine if this would be a good fit for our
program.

-- 37 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 31)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
39
IX. Four-Year Action Plan – Resource Allocation Request (RAR)
Fortunately, CTE has been able to rely on Strong Work Force (SWF) funding to improve and
expand our program. By leveraging this funding, we have not had to request any RARs in the
last couple of years. We have concerns moving forward that the College of the Siskiyous is
relying on this STW funding always being present to support CTE. We would like to see some
long-term planning to fund the positions needed without using SWF funds.
A. Facilities
• Apparatus Bay
Immediate need for a new and larger Apparatus Bay to store all the public safety
equipment. This would also include space for EMS and Police CTE equipment.
• Dorms for CTE Public Safety Students
Students have addressed concerns regarding sharing housing with students of separate
programs on different schedules. These concerns highlight the distractions of studying
and quiet hours are described as regularly violated causing issues with rest.
• Public Safety Area
All the public safety Coordinators have agreed that it would be extremely beneficial for
our programs to be located in the same area. Merging our programs into a localized
area on campus will free up vital space on campus that often conflicts with other
activities and support our students who will start to learn how to work together in a
public safety team mentality.
• Training Grounds
Continue to expand our training grounds with new and upgraded props.
• Fire Station
This would be a joint program with the local Fire Departments. This would support a
working Fire Station on Campus that would provide on-the-job training to our students
along with providing a higher level of EMS and Fire protection to our Campus and the
local area. This could lower our insurance payment for the college and be a great
recruiting tool to draw students to our program. We recommend exploring certain
organizations that currently have this infrastructure in place to identify if this will be a
good fit for our program.

-- 38 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 32)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
40
B. Administration
• Instructor Fire Academy
Adding a full-time instructor to manage the Fire Academy
• Curriculum
Continue to work on updating the curriculum in eLumen to stay current with the State
Fire Training update cycle.
• Instructional Support Specialist
This full-time CTE position was tentatively approved but has yet to be announced or
filled. Primary duties would be to take care of all CTE, equipment, and props.
• Associate’s Degree in Wildland Fire
Investigate and research the feasibility of adding a new Associate’s Degree track in
Wildland Fire. This is being researched by the California Fire Technology Directors
Association. Course plans have been developed and beta programs are being run.
C. Equipment/Props
• Weed Tower – Fire Props Upgrade
The Class B-Propane Fire Props are broken and out of date, and parts are no longer
available. They need to be upgraded to a new modern system after 20-plus years of use.
Fire Technology worked with Pacific Power on a grant opportunity to fund this project.
Unfortunately, we did not receive this grant funding.
Prepared and Submitted By:
Chris Delcour Fire Technology Coordinator/Instructor
Jesse Roberts Fire Technology Instructor Date: Oct. 31, 2024

41
Appendix A - COSFT Employment Information
The College of the Siskiyou’s Fire Technology “COSFT” has 2 positions that can be applied for based on
the following criteria. As a State Fire Training approved ARTP we must also follow State Fire Training
Regulations. Potential instructors must be qualified under both College of the Siskiyou’s & State Fire
Training rules and regulations. Below is a condensed list of the requirements.
The 2 positions available to apply for at the College of the Siskiyous are:
1. Part-Time Adjunct Instructor Job Application
6 years experience in the Fire Service
2-year Degree or higher
2. Expert Instructional Aid “EIA” Job Application
Please email me at cdelcour@siskiyous.edu once you have completed your online
application.
State Fire Training Minimum Requirements to teach at the Fire Academy.
The information below is from the State Fire Training Bulletin on July 22, 2029.
FIRE FIGHTER I / II ACADEMY INSTRUCTOR REQUIREMENTS
Starting January 1, 2019, new instructors for Fire Fighter I and Fire Fighter II academies will be
required to meet the following requirements:
• Instructor I Certification (40 Hours Instructional Experience and completed Task Book)
• Ethical Leadership in the Classroom
• Fire Fighter 1 Certification
• Fire Fighter 2 Certification – to teach those skills
Current Academy Instructors (those who were instructing at the ALA/ARTP on/before
December 31, 2018) maintain their status even though they may not be “Certified Instructors.”
To qualify for this exemption, you must have a “Fire Fighter 1 & 2 Instructor” listed in your Acadis
certifications. SFT runs a quarterly report looking for the above certifications and then adds this
certification automatically to your Acadis.

-- 40 of 58 --

title: Fire Technology PROGRAM REVIEW 23-24 Final (Part 33)
text:
2023-2024 Fire Technology
Program Review
42
INFORMATIONAL BULLETIN
Instructor Changes
Issued: July 22, 2019
State Fire Training (SFT) proposed new instructor requirements and terminology that was
approved by Statewide Training and Education Advisory Committee (STEAC), July 2017, and
approved by State Board of Fire Services (SBFS), August 2017.
The instructor qualifications used to become a registered instructor and certified instructor has
been utilized since the 1980s within SFT. These approved changes will enhance the professional
qualifications of new future SFT instructors while utilizing current Registered instructors that
are in good standing with SFT.
CURRENT INSTRUCTOR REQUIREMENTS
Instructor Registration remains unchanged through December 31, 2018. Instructor registration
currently requires the following:
• Instructor Courses fulfilled from one of the following training qualifications:
o Attended and passed SFT Instructor I and Instructor II OR
o Attended and passed SFT Training Instructor 1A, 1B, and 1C OR
o Attended and passed SFT Training Instructor 1A,1B, and Instructor II OR
o Attended and passed SFT Fire Instructor 1A and 1B and Training Instructor 1C or Fire
Instructor 2A OR
o Attended and passed Fire Instructor 1A,1B, and Instructor II OR
o Holds a valid lifetime community college teaching credential
o Completed the 60-hour Techniques of Teaching course from a University of
California (UC) or California State University (CSU) institution OR
o Completed the National Fire Academy’s (NFA) Fire Service Instructional
Methodology course
o Completed four semester units of upper-division credit in educational materials,
methods, or curriculum development
• Regional Instructor Orientation (RIO) completed within the previous two (2) years of the
application date
• Ethical Leadership in the Classroom and has a signed SFT Instructor Code of Ethics on file
• 80 Hours Instructional Experience
CFTES / FSTEP INSTRUCTOR REQUIREMENTS
Starting January 1, 2019, new instructors applying to teach CFSTES / FSTEP courses will be
required to meet the following requirements:
• Instructor I Certification (40 Hours Instructional Experience and completed Task Book)
• Instructor II Certification (40 Hours Instructional Experience and completed Task Book)
• Regional Instructor Orientation (RIO) completed within two (2) years of application date
Published: November 2019 State Fire Training Page 1 of 2

54
Appendix J – Equipment / Prop Purchased
Upgrading the outdated equipment and props for the College of the Siskiyous Fire Technology Program
is essential to ensure that students receive the most effective and realistic training possible. Modern
firefighting techniques and technologies have evolved significantly. Using modern equipment will
better prepare students for real-world scenarios. Updated tools and props not only enhance the
learning experience but also improve safety during training exercises. By investing in state-of-the-art
equipment, the program can maintain its high standards and reputation, attract more students, and
ultimately produce more competent and confident firefighters ready to serve
their communities.
• Lion Attack Digital Fire Training Simulator
o The ATTACK™ Digital Fire Training System combines digital flames, sound and
smoke to create a range of fire conditions that respond directly to hose line
application.
• Smoke machine
o Creates realistic smoke-filled training environments without exposing trainees to
dangerous conditions
• New Fire Nozzles
o Updated our nozzles to modern Fire Departments use.
• New hand tools
o Fire fighter Axes, hooks, belts, lights, and other assortment of hand
tools
• Fire Hose – Structure and Wildland hose
• CTE Pick Up Truck –
o Dodge 3500 pickup truck, used by all CTE as a shared resource.
• Rope Rescue Equipment
• Rescue Dummies
o Added realistic victim dummies that simulate a real human weight and
movement
• Fixed Stand Pipe in Weed Tower
• Replace the damage wood floor in Weed Tower. 4 floors of plywood, the
labor was completed by Cal Fire Crews.
• 50 Scott SCBA
o Donations by city of Berkeley Fire Department, estimated to over
$50,000. This will lower the cost of equipment rentals to our
Cadets by $600
• Extra Connex’s for more storage
• Forcible Entry Doors and Windows
o Allows us to simulate forcing doors and windows open.
• Chain Saws
• Thermal Imaging Camaras

-- 53 of 58 --

55
• Pelican LED Scene Lights
• Pelican Firefighter Light Box’s
• DeWalt battery-powered Tool’s
• CTE - Dump Trailer
o We had a remarkably excessive cost on trash disposal by renting large dumpsters.
We now can quickly do trash or recycle run to the transfer station. We estimate
that this will pay off in less than one year.
• Lockers
o Students will have a locker room for the first time in the Fire Academy History
• Auto extrication simulator prop
o Supports IFSAC & ProBoard Testing
• FireVent Roof Ventilation Prop
o Creates a safe and adjustable platform to perform ventilation skills
• New Flash Over Box
o This prop allows the student to witness real fire behavior, and it can be controlled.
